data.title: Mamdani says Iran war 'should be opposed on every single ground'
data.description:
New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani (D) expressed deep disapproval of the U.S.-Israeli conflict against Iran in an interview released Wednesday.

“It’s a war that should be opposed on every single ground, not just procedural, but also moral, but also political, and also a war that should be opposed on the grounds of the fact that it is being financed by the very money that could make it easier to keep calling this city, this country, home, and yet, there is a blindness to that fact,” Mamdani told political commentator Brian Tyler Cohen in the interview.

At the beginning of the conflict against Iran, Mamdani called U.S. and Israeli strikes “a catastrophic escalation in an illegal war of aggression.”

“Bombing cities. Killing civilians. Opening a new theater of war. Americans do not want this. They do not want another war in pursuit of regime change. They want relief from the affordability crisis. They want peace,” the New York mayor added in a post on the social platform X.

President Trump, with whom Mamdani has had a cordial relationship in recent months, has lately considered a deployment of American troops on Iranian soil to take the country’s highly enriched uranium — an operation which could last for days.

The operation would put U.S. personnel far inside Iran, leaving them vulnerable to Iranian short-range missiles and drones.

On Tuesday, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said that the president will address the nation on the ongoing conflict with Iran on Wednesday night.

“TUNE IN: Tomorrow night at 9PM ET, President Trump will give an Address to the Nation to provide an important update on Iran,” Leavitt said in a Tuesday evening post on X.

data.title: Trump to attend Supreme Court oral arguments for birthright citizenship case
data.description:
President Trump is set to attend Wednesday’s Supreme Court hearing on birthright citizenship, according to his public schedule, making him the first sitting president to attend oral arguments at the nation’s highest court.

Before his schedule was released, the president told reporters in the Oval Office on Tuesday that he planned to attend.

“I’m going,” Trump said, NBC News reported. “Because I have listened to this argument for so long.”

On the first day back in office last year, the president signed an executive order restricting birthright citizenship to citizens and legal permanent residents, awhich quickly drew backlash in courts across the country. It has yet to go into effect as the battle has raged in the courts for more than a year.

A series of lower judges have repeatedly ruled Trump’s directive violates the 14th Amendment’s Citizenship Clause, which states that people born in the U.S. and “subject to the jurisdiction thereof” are citizens.

The Supreme Court is set to hear arguments in the case on Wednesday. Some of the conservative justices, along with the three liberals, have already signaled their opposition to Trump’s order.

data.title: Chemicals in plastics linked to newborn deaths, premature births: Research
data.description:
Two chemicals commonly used to make plastics more flexible are connected to newborn deaths and premature births, according to a study released Monday.

According to the study, published in the journal eClinicalMedicine, two chemicals, di(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) and diisononyl phthalate (DINP), were notably linked to newborn deaths and premature births.

The study looked at data from 2018, estimating close to 1.97 million worldwide premature births in connection with DEHP, or 8.74 percent of all premature births. DEHP was also estimated to be linked to 74,000 newborn deaths, per the study.

DINP was also estimated to be linked to 1.88 million premature births and 64,000 newborn deaths in 2018.

An analysis from last spring also found that daily exposure to the same type of chemicals could be linked to over 350,000 cardiovascular-related deaths in 2018. The chemicals, called phthalates, are often used in personal care products, children’s toys and food packaging and processing materials.

Phthalates have been known to disrupt hormone function and have been linked to birth defects, infertility, learning disabilities and neurological disorders. 

“As treaty negotiations to regulate phthalates move forward, further estimation of the vast costs of the plastics industry are needed, and strong regulatory measures to phthalates as a class should be considered,” the study published Tuesday reads.

data.title: Leavitt: Trump to address the nation with Iran update
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said Wednesday that President Trump will address the nation on the ongoing conflict with Iran on Thursday night.

“TUNE IN: Tomorrow night at 9PM ET, President Trump will give an Address to the Nation to provide an important update on Iran,” Leavitt said in a Wednesday evening post on the social platform X.

The U.S.-Israeli conflict against Iran recently stretched into its second month, with recent polling showing disapproval of the fighting. Trump has been considering a deployment of American troops on Iranian ground to take the country’s highly enriched uranium, an operation that could last for days.

The mission would most likely be carried out by elite special operations to extract radioactive material. It would also put U.S. personnel far inside Iranian territory, leaving them vulnerable to Iranian short-range missiles and drones.

On Tuesday, the U.S. military attacked an Iranian city where one of the country’s main nuclear sites is located. Last June, the Isfahan Nuclear Energy Center was among three facilities that were hit by U.S. B-2 bombers and a submarine. According to The Associated Press, analysts think the majority of Iran’s highly enriched uranium is likely at the site.

On Monday, Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baqaei said that while Iranian officials had not negotiated with the Trump administration since the beginning of the conflict, the U.S. had submitted a “negotiation request.”

“We have had no negotiations with America in these thirty-one days,” Baqaei said in a briefing. “What has occurred is the submission of a negotiation request, accompanied by a set of proposals from America, which has reached us through certain intermediaries, including Pakistan.”

data.title: Hegseth lifts suspension for Army pilots who did fly-by at Kid Rock's estate
data.description:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth on Tuesday announced that the Army crew that flew two AH‑64 Apache helicopters near singer Kid Rock’s Nashville, Tenn., estate over the weekend will not face disciplinary measures or an investigation.

The military personnel had been suspended for their actions, a spokesperson confirmed to The Hill on Tuesday, but that suspension was lifted later in the day by Hegseth.

“Thank you @KidRock. @USArmy pilots suspension LIFTED. No punishment. No investigation. Carry on, patriots,” Hegseth said in a post Tuesday on the social platform X.

The helicopters, which were from the 101st Combat Aviation Brigade at Fort Campbell, Kentucky, flew in the Nashville area Saturday, including near the home of Robert James Ritchie, known as Kid Rock, near a residence he owns and has dubbed “The Southern White House.” 

“The personnel involved have been suspended from flight duties while the Army reviews the circumstances surrounding the mission, including compliance with relevant FAA regulations, aviation safety protocol and approval requirements,” Maj. Montrell Russell, an Army spokesperson, said in a statement earlier Tuesday before Hegseth’s announcement.

Russell said that because there was an active investigation, which Hegseth has since lifted, it would have been “inappropriate to discuss specific findings at this time,” adding that the Army “takes any allegations of unauthorized or unsafe flight operations very seriously and is committed to enforcing standards and holding personnel accountable.” 

Rock posted a video of the encounter on the X with the caption, “This is a level of respect that s‑‑‑ for brains Governor of California will never know. God Bless America and all those who have made the ultimate sacrifice to defend her.”

The singer, who earlier this year performed at an alternate halftime performance in protest of Bad Bunny’s Super Bowl show, is an ally of Trump.

Filip Timotija contributed.

data.title: How Trump's complex operation to extract Iran's enriched uranium could unfold
data.description:
President Trump is mulling a deployment of U.S. troops on the ground in Iran to remove the country’s highly enriched uranium, a complex and dangerous operation that could take days to complete. 

The mission, which would likely involve elite special operations teams to extract radioactive material, would place U.S. personnel deep inside Iran, exposing them to the country’s short-range missiles and drones and running the risk of prolonging the month-old conflict. 

The operation would be one of the most dangerous missions greenlighted by Trump, requiring U.S. service members to fly to the sites and secure their perimeters. Specialists would then have to remove the material buried deep underground — facilities that were compromised by bunker-buster bombs launched in joint U.S.-Israeli strikes last June — and have it transferred to safe locations. They would have to do this while likely sustaining fire from Iran’s military, according to former defense officials and experts, some of whom warned the mission could take at least several days to finish. 

“It’s not going to be like swinging by a warehouse, grabbing something with a forklift, putting it on a plane, leaving. This is going to be something of extreme complexity, and that will take a significant amount of time,” said retired U.S. Army Col. Seth Krummrich. 

CIA Director John Ratcliffe said earlier this month that Iran currently has at least 440 kilograms of 60 percent enriched, weapons-grade uranium that would allow it to build 10 nuclear weapons.

Since the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran’s premier nuclear sites — Natanz, Fordow and Isfahan — Iran has not been able to enrich “a single kilogram” of uranium to 60 percent, Ratcliffe said during a recent hearing before the House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence, adding that the U.S. intelligence community believes that Tehran did not give up on its ambitions to build a nuclear weapon. 

The material is mainly located in Isfahan, though some of it is also in Natanz, according to the director of the International Atomic Energy Agency, Rafael Grossi. 

“The impression we have, and I think that is a widespread impression in others who are also observing, is that it hasn’t been moved,” Grossi told reporters earlier this month during his visit to Washington, D.C. 

Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard told House lawmakers earlier this month that the intelligence community has “high confidence” that they know where Tehran’s enriched uranium is located.

If the Trump administration decides to undertake such a maneuver, military planners will need to take into account the distance to get to the sites, the condition the material might be in and the types of specialized equipment needed to extract it — all signs that the size of the U.S. force involved will need to be “quite large,” according to retired Navy Adm. Gary Roughead. 

“In my view, I think you’re not talking dozens,” Roughead said in an interview with The Hill when asked about the size of the force that will need to be involved. “Then on top of that, it’s what is the air support that you would want to have available, and then what protection do you have to put in around the site, or sites where those who are specialized in dealing with this sort of material are going to be working. So, it seems to me that it would be a pretty sizable force.”

Steven Wills, a navalist at the Center for Maritime Strategy, argued that the Army, Air Force and Navy’s elite military units could take part in the mission, emphasizing that the Air Force personnel “at a minimum” would be needed for setting up airstrips. 

Retired Vice Adm. Robert Harward, who served as the deputy commander of U.S. Central Command (Centcom), said the U.S. could use the Joint Special Operations Command forces, including Army Rangers, which have recently been deployed to the Middle East; rely on the 101st Airborne, an elite light infantry division known for its air assault operations; or utilize the combination of both since they have “worked together before, and that probably brings your biggest scale to it.” 

Marine Expeditionary Units (MEU), two of which have been order to the Centcom region in recent weeks, that are designed for amphibious operations, could also partake in the operation, but they have not “trained as specifically as those forces are, and they’re not airborne qualified, so you’d have to seize some territory to get them in somewhere,” Harward said in an interview with The Hill. 

Krummrich, now the vice president of Global Guardian, agreed with Harward that the 101st Airborne, Army Rangers and 82nd Airborne, an Army airborne infantry division that specializes in parachute assaults, are all capable of securing the site, but noted that other levers of the U.S. military need to participate, not just ground forces.

“There would be constant close air support, there would be satellite support, there would be every possible spectrum of warfare, defense and offense available to keep any sort of enemy activity away from the sensitive site to allow the Joint Special Operation command and some other agencies to be able to go through and methodically and deliberately excavate the site, retrieve the nuclear materials and then also confirm that they got everything,” Krummrich said. 

Even if U.S. forces go into Isfahan and Natanz, the administration needs to decide if it will go get the 60 percent enriched uranium, or also try to secure the 20 percent variant, or even the 5 and 3.5 percent low-enriched uranium that Tehran also has on hand, said Eric Brewer, the deputy vice president of Nuclear Materials Security Program. 

“There’s also a risk that Iran could have deployed fakes, decoys, essentially, to make that task of removal a lot harder,” Brewer said in an interview. “And there’s always a chance that you miss something, right? There’s always a chance that, let’s say, you pinpoint the two locations you think it’s that you go down there, and you only find half of what you expect.” 

The process of getting people inside the tunnels to handle the material, digging out all the rock that’s buried in there and the size of the enriched uranium, which is believed to be housed in dozens of special cylinders, which would need to be transported out, could stretch the intricate operation from several days to weeks, according to former defense officials. 

“It could be as short as a day, but I would assume, with the tunnels and all the things and what they’ve done to prepare for it, it would probably take longer,” Harward, the executive vice president for International Business and Strategy at Shield AI, said. 

Krummrich said the operation would be the opposite of a snatch-and-grab as it will have to be a “very deliberate excavation done not only by engineers, but by scientists and very specialized special operations forces that have been trained to be able to do this sort of nuclear material retrieval, and it’s not an easy job.”  

“Under the best circumstances, it would take days or a week, depending on having to secure the site,” he said. 

Wills, of the Center for Maritime Strategy, said there will need to be a lot of refueling to take place and utilization of electronic warfare to jam Iran’s radars, but said the mission could be “doable in terms of the package, but getting in there and holding it long enough to extract whatever materials you need to could be a challenge. It depends on how accessible they are.” 

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the U.S. military is looking “to be unpredictable” in placing troops on the ground in Iran.  

“We’re not going to foreclose any option,” Hegseth said during a Pentagon briefing Tuesday morning. 

“You can’t fight and win a war if you tell your adversary what you are willing to do or what you are not willing to do — to include boots on the ground,” the Pentagon chief told reporters. 

Since the conflict with Iran broke out in late February, 348 U.S. service members have been wounded and 315 of them have already returned to duty, Centcom spokesperson, U.S. Navy Capt. Tim Hawkins, told The Hill on Tuesday. 

Harward, the former Centcom commander, argued the high stakes of the mission and the Iranian military knowing the U.S. is coming just does not make sense to execute it now. 

“Focus on the other things. First, if you stop the missiles and you can open up the Strait of Hormuz, you can take the time to do whatever you want, and as the Israelis are adamantly pursuing, they’re going after regime change,” he told The Hill. “Well, if you get off those conditions, why take the risk? At that point, you could go get the uranium with no risk.”

Krummrich said he has a hard time seeing the mission unfold unless there is an initial deal struck between Washington and Tehran that would allow for a “semi-permissive environment for this force to come in and do the nuclear material extraction.” 

“You would need all of the protective equipment if any of this nuclear material was compromised and exposed, because that means everyone who handled it would have the risk of being poisoned, and anyone around that specific area,” he said. “So then you’ve got to have the ability to contain the material once you find it. And the containment process is critical. I mean, it’s, frankly, probably one of the most important parts. Find it and then you’ve got to contain it safely, and then you’ve got to transport it.”

Even if the personnel get the material out of the ground, all of the surveillance, engineering and  protective equipment is needed to package and transport it, Krummrich said, adding that “once you package it up, you still have the decontamination potential that you’re going to have to have for all of the helicopters, the planes, the people that touch it and around it, the sites itself.”  

“It’s an extremely thorough and painstaking process that you can’t rush,” he said.

data.title: The Memo: Trump struggles to claim victory as he edges toward ending Iran war
data.description:
President Trump looks to be edging toward ending the war on Iran, which is now in its fifth week. 

“We’ll be leaving very soon,” the president told reporters in the Oval Office late Tuesday afternoon.

But it will be hard even for someone with Trump’s penchant for self-praise to claim victory unless some fundamental realities change before hostilities cease.

The de facto closure of the Strait of Hormuz has caused oil prices to soar, an increase that has been mirrored in gas prices at the pump for Americans. The average price per gallon for regular gas surpassed $4 Tuesday, according to data from AAA.

The former supreme leader of Iran, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, was killed on the first day of the war by an Israeli airstrike, but he has been succeeded by his son, Mojtaba Khamenei. The new supreme leader has not been seen in public, whether for security reasons or because he is rumored to be injured. But the Islamic Republic itself has shown no imminent signs of collapse.

In addition, Iran still holds the enriched uranium  — “nuclear dust,” as Trump sometimes refers to it — which it had at the start of the war. 

The difficulty for the president is that changing any of these key factors would be an extremely risky endeavor.

In particular, seizing the uranium would require an extremely complicated military operation, barring some negotiated deal where Iran would agree to the removal of the material from its land. 

The material is likely split between at least two sites, deeply underground and fortified. A Wall Street Journal investigation in recent days noted that a mission to seize the uranium would have several different and challenging stages and that it likely “would take days or even a week to complete.” 

Such a mission would, by its very nature, carry an appreciable risk of the loss of American life. 

Were that to happen, it would likely make an already-unpopular war even more so. 

It would also invite a comparison that would appall Trump. Almost exactly 46 years ago, a mission approved by then-President Carter to rescue American hostages from Iran ended in ignominy when a U.S. helicopter crashed into a transport plane in the desert, killing eight service members.

Forcing a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz would scarcely be easier, since the narrow body of water lends itself to attacks on shipping that can be made quickly from the Iranian shore. 

Even Trump acknowledged this in his Tuesday remarks, saying, “You can take a machine gun from the shore, shoot a little few bullets at a ship, or maybe an over-the-shoulder missile.”

It is possible that the U.S. could put troops on the ground to try to secure the area, but that would once again bring obvious risk to life and would not necessarily succeed. 

More to the point, Trump seems increasingly resigned to the idea of being unable to wrest back control of the strait, instead arguing it is up to allies to do so. 

In an angry Tuesday morning social media post, he contended that nations including the United Kingdom could buy oil from the U.S. or “build up some delayed courage, go to the Strait and just TAKE IT.”

As for the regime itself, it is always possible it could begin to teeter under the attritional effects of the U.S. and Israeli assault that began on Feb. 28. 

At a Pentagon briefing Tuesday morning, Gen. Dan Caine, the chair of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, asserted that more than 11,000 Iranian targets had been hit. Caine also asserted that an “increase in air superiority” had enabled B-52 bombers to start operating over Iranian territory.

For the moment, however, Trump and key aides are asserting that regime change has already taken place. 

Trump made this claim on Air Force One over the weekend, saying “We’ve had regime change, if you look already, because the one regime was decimated.”

Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth said Tuesday that regime change had “occurred” in Iran.

The events in Iran so far do not meet the conventional definition of regime change.

The challenges that now surround the operation in Iran — and its domestic ramifications — are causing at least some degree of consternation even among Republicans.

GOP strategist Ron Bonjean said he hoped that the administration would “accomplish their military objectives as quickly as possible and start focusing on the affordability issues” that matter at home.

However, Bonjean argued it shouldn’t be taken as a given that Trump was going to pull back on Iran. 

He noted the thousands of troops from both the Marines and the 82nd Airborne Division who have been deployed to the region.

“I think you should watch what the U.S. military is doing rather than what President Trump is saying,” he urged.

However, there are real hurdles when it comes to public opinion if Trump wishes to extend the war.

A poll last week from Quinnipiac University found that 59 percent of registered voters disapproved of how he is handling the situation in Iran, against 34 percent who approved. 

In terms of overall job approval, polling expert Nate Silver noted Monday that Trump’s approval had dipped below 40 percent for the first time in his second term.

Todd Belt, the director of the political management program at the George Washington University, said that Trump would struggle to win over all but his most loyal supporters to the idea that the war has been worthwhile.

“Outside the MAGA base, as long as the gas prices stay high, it’s not going to look like a victory,” Belt said. “People are going to say, ‘Why did we do this, what did we achieve?’”

The Memo is a reported column by Niall Stanage.

data.title: Bipartisan senators unveil measure targeting rural housing costs
data.description:
A bipartisan group of senators unveiled legislation Tuesday aimed at making it easier for people living in rural areas to buy homes, as affordability remains top of mind for lawmakers and voters heading into the midterms.  

The legislation, first reported by NBC News, would help nearly 30 million homebuyers qualify for rural housing assistance for the first time by modernizing loan eligibility requirements. It is sponsored by Sens. Peter Welch (D-Vt.), Jim Justice (R-W.Va.), Adam Schiff (D-Calif.) and Cindy Hyde-Smith (R-Miss.).

“We’re in the midst of an affordable housing crisis, and Vermonters are amongst the hardest hit,” Welch said in a statement. “Making farm credit loans more accessible is vital to addressing this crisis and will help millions of rural families experience the joy of homeownership for the first time.”

Farm credit institutions are currently only allowed to lend to homebuyers who are living in areas with populations of 2,500 or less under the definition of “rural areas” outlined in the Farm Credit Act of 1971.

The proposed measure would increase the population limit from 2,500 to 10,000 inhabitants, a change lawmakers said aligns closer with the U.S. Department of Agriculture’s (USDA) rural housing program eligibility requirements.

The move would open a pathway to housing assistance to nearly 40 percent of the U.S. population, according to lawmakers.

“All over rural America, folks just want to own a home, raise their families, and build something that lasts,” Justice said in a statement. “This bill helps increase competition for credit and opens the door for over 230,000 West Virginians to do just that.”

The proposal comes after a Redfin analysis released in November found that housing unaffordability is accelerating more rapidly in rural areas than in bigger cities and suburbs, as rising prices outpace wages.  

The real estate company found that the median sale price for a rural home rose nearly 61 percent from before the COVID-19 pandemic to $280,900. Someone hoping to purchase a home in a rural county now needs to earn almost $75,000 annually to afford a median-priced home, a 105.8 percent jump from the third quarter of 2019.  

“Buying a home is one of the great promises of the American Dream, but for too many, that promise is out of reach. We need to fix the housing crisis and break down the barriers that hold rural families back,” said Rep. Kristen McDonald Rivet (D-Mich.), who introduced companion legislation in the House alongside Rep. Bill Huizenga (R-Mich.).

The companion bills would build on the comprehensive, bipartisan housing package passed by the Senate earlier this month, which included incentives to build new homes, establish a program to convert abandoned buildings into housing developments and authorize new grants to modernize existing homes, among other provisions. It has stalled in the House.

Meanwhile, the Trump administration has also unveiled several affordability proposals in recent months as part of a broader economic messaging push ahead of November.

President Trump directed Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac in January to buy $200 billion of mortgage bonds to lower rates and ordered a ban on large institutional investors buying single-family homes in February. He also signed an executive order earlier this month that seeks to remove regulatory barriers in construction.  

Administration officials have also floated the possibility of allowing Americans to dip into their 401(k) plans for a down payment on a home without penalties, though Trump has distanced himself from that idea.

data.title: Springsteen launching 'political and very topical' tour in Minneapolis
data.description:
Rock legend Bruce Springsteen is kicking off the American leg of what he called a “political and very topical” tour on Tuesday in Minneapolis, Minn.

“The tour is going to be political and very topical about what’s going on in the country,” Springsteen said last Monday, according to the Minnesota Star Tribune, describing his “Land Of Hope And Dreams American Tour.”

“Minneapolis and St. Paul, that was the place I wanted to begin it, and I wanted to end it in Washington.”

Springsteen, who has criticized President Trump and his administration in the past, released an anti-Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) song earlier this year following the fatal shootings of Alex Pretti and Renee Good in Minneapolis. 

“I wrote this song on Saturday, recorded it yesterday and released it to you today in response to the state terror being visited on the city of Minneapolis,” Springsteen said in an Instagram post in January.

“It’s dedicated to the people of Minneapolis, our innocent immigrant neighbors and in memory of Alex Pretti and Renee Good,” he added.

The Star Tribune also previously reported that Springsteen was set to perform at a “No Kings” demonstration last weekend at the state Capitol in St. Paul, Minn.

“You want to try to meet the moment,” Springsteen said, per the outlet. “The No Kings movement is of great import right now.”

“When you have the opportunity to sing something where the timing is essential and if you have something powerful to sing, it elevates the moment, it elevates your job to another level. And I’m always in search of that.”

data.title: Trump says US will be done with Iran in 2-3 weeks
data.description:
President Trump said Tuesday that the United States will leave Iran within two to three weeks, a longer deadline than the four to six weeks the administration has been saying.

“I would say within two weeks, maybe three,” Trump said in the Oval Office during an executive order signing. “We’re hitting them very hard. Last night we knocked out tremendous amounts of missile-making facilities.”

But he said, “It’s possible that we’ll make a deal before that.”

“If they come to the table, that’ll be good. But it doesn’t matter whether they come or not, we’ve set them back,” he said.

The administration has repeatedly said the U.S.’s operation in Iran will last four to six weeks. It is currently in its fifth week.

Trump said he had one goal with the military operation, which was getting Iran to not have a nuclear weapon, “and that goal has been attained.”

When asked what his plan was to bring down gas prices, which have risen since the start of the operation in Iran because of the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, Trump said, “All I have to do is leave Iran.”

“They’ll come tumbling down,” he said.

The strait usually sees the passage of 20 million barrels of oil and petroleum products a day, but supply has been cut off except for a limited number of ships.

Trump said, however, that he “heard tremendous numbers of ships were sailing through” and that negotiations continue.
